voice: Hushed, exacting, and warm — a photographer who talks about light and quiet moments rather than packages and deliverables.

designTokens.paletteMood: Near-black gallery walls, warm bone-white light, and a single restrained thread of muted gold.

title.value: Weddings remembered in low, honest light

subtitle.value: Marlow & Fen is a fine-art wedding photographer based in the Hudson Valley, photographing intimate ceremonies and long-table dinners across the Northeast on film and available light.

data.title: A quiet year of weddings
data.subtitle: Twelve frames pulled from farmhouse ceremonies, city hall mornings, and one rain-soaked vineyard on the Cayuga shore.

data.quote: We forgot the camera was there, and then a month later we were crying at our kitchen table over a photo of my grandmother laughing. That is the whole thing, isn't it.

data.title: I am Ren Marlow, and I shoot toward the edges of the day
data.description: I photograph roughly eighteen weddings a year so that each one gets my full attention — the slow morning light, the toast that goes long, the last dance when the room finally softens. I work mostly on medium-format film with a digital body for the dark hours, and I stay out of the way until the moment asks me not to.
items: Based in Kingston, NY; traveling across the Hudson Valley, Catskills, and greater New York
Medium-format film and available light, with discreet flash reserved for the dance floor
One photographer, one unhurried day — never a rotating team of shooters

data.title: Tell me about your day
data.description: Share the date, the place, and a little of how the two of you found each other. I answer every inquiry personally within two business days, and I only book one wedding per weekend.
data.successMessage: Thank you — your note landed with me. I will write back personally within two business days.

data.title: Full weddings, start to last light
data.subtitle: A longer look at recent days — getting-ready rooms, ceremonies, the private minutes between, and the dinners that ran past midnight.

data.title: Eleven years behind the same eye

items.label: Weddings taken each year, no more

data.title: How I photograph a wedding
data.subtitle: Every collection is built around the same unhurried approach; the only thing that changes is how much of the day we spend together.

items.title: Pre-wedding walk
items.description: We meet at your venue or a place that matters to the two of you, shoot for an hour, and get the nerves out long before the wedding day so the camera already feels familiar.

items.title: Full-day coverage
items.description: From the quiet of getting ready through the last song, I document the whole arc on medium-format film with a digital body held back for the dark reception hours.

items.title: Hand-edited film scans
items.description: Every frame is culled and color-graded by hand to hold warm skin tones and true blacks; I never batch-run presets or outsource the edit to anyone else.

items.title: Heirloom albums
items.description: Layflat albums are printed on cotton stock and bound in linen or leather in a small bindery in Beacon, sequenced by me so the book reads like the day actually felt.

items.title: Second shooter, on request
items.description: For larger weddings I bring one trusted second photographer so the ceremony is covered from two angles without turning your day into a crowd of cameras.

items.title: Private online gallery
items.description: Your finished images arrive in a password-protected gallery within four weeks, with print-ready files and a simple way to share favorites with family.

data.title: What it felt like to work together
data.subtitle: A few notes from couples whose weddings I photographed over the last two seasons.

items.name: Sofia & Dara
items.quote: Ren caught my grandmother laughing during the toast — a frame none of us saw happen — and it is now the largest print in our apartment. He is invisible in the best way.

items.name: Marcus & Theo
items.quote: We had ninety minutes and a courthouse full of strangers. Ren turned that chaos into the most tender set of photos, then walked us to a diner for the last few frames. Perfect.

items.name: Priya & Anand
items.quote: It poured for our ceremony and I panicked. Ren just smiled and shot the rain, and those grey, wet, glowing photos are the ones we love most now.

items.name: Elena & Jack
items.quote: The film scans are unreal — warm and soft and true to how the light actually looked at golden hour. Our album from the Beacon bindery is a genuine heirloom.

data.title: Three ways to spend the day together
data.subtitle: Every collection includes hand-edited film scans and a private gallery; the difference is how many hours we have and what you take home in print.

tiers.name: The Elopement
tiers.description: For city hall mornings and small ceremonies of twenty or fewer, start to a late lunch.
features: Up to five hours of coverage
One photographer, medium-format film and digital

tiers.name: The Full Day
tiers.description: My most-booked collection — getting ready through the last dance, unhurried.
features: Up to ten hours of coverage
Hand-edited film scans and private gallery
10x10 linen layflat album from the Beacon bindery
Complimentary travel within the Hudson Valley

tiers.name: The Weekend
tiers.description: For welcome dinners, ceremonies, and the slow morning-after breakfast.
features: Two days of coverage, up to sixteen hours total

data.quote: I would rather miss a posed smile than a real one. So I stay quiet, I wait for the light, and I trust that the day already knows what it wants to be.

data.title: Only one wedding a weekend, and dates go early
data.subtitle: If your date feels right, tell me about it — I answer every inquiry myself within two business days.

data.title: Let's see if your date is open
data.description: Tell me the date, the venue if you have one, and a little about how the two of you found each other. I book one wedding per weekend, so early notes are best. Every message reaches me directly and I reply within two business days.
data.successMessage: Your note is with me — thank you. I will write back personally within two business days to talk dates and next steps.

data.title: The questions couples ask first
data.subtitle: A few honest answers about how I work, what things cost, and how far I travel.

items.question: How many weddings do you take each year?
items.answer: Eighteen at most, and only one per weekend. I turn work away to keep this pace, which is why I ask couples to reach out as early as they can — popular fall Saturdays in the Hudson Valley are usually gone twelve to fifteen months ahead.

items.question: Where are you based and do you travel?
items.answer: I live in Kingston, New York and photograph across the Hudson Valley, the Catskills, and greater New York with no travel fee. For weddings elsewhere in the Northeast, travel is included in The Weekend collection and quoted simply for the others.

items.question: Do you really shoot film?
items.answer: Yes — most of the day is medium-format film for its warmth and forgiving skin tones. I carry a digital body for the ceremony if the church is dark and for the reception once the sun is down, so nothing is ever missed to grain or low light.

items.question: What do collections cost and what's required to book?
items.answer: Collections run from $3,200 for an elopement to $8,900 for a two-day weekend, all with hand-edited film scans and a private gallery. A signed agreement and a one-third retainer hold your date; the balance is due two weeks before the wedding.

items.question: When will we see our photos?
items.answer: A small preview set of ten to fifteen images arrives within one week of the wedding. Your full private gallery — every finished, hand-graded frame — is ready within four weeks, and albums follow four to six weeks after you approve the layout.

items.question: Do you bring a second photographer?
items.answer: A second shooter is included in The Weekend collection and available as an add-on for the others. For most weddings under 120 guests I work alone on purpose, because one quiet photographer disappears into the day far better than two.

items.question: What happens if you're sick or something goes wrong?
items.answer: I carry two camera bodies and back up every card the same night to two drives. In the rare case I truly cannot photograph your day, I draw on a small trusted network of Hudson Valley film photographers to cover it and still edit the final images myself.

data.title: Or just say hello
data.subtitle: If you would rather write directly or ring the studio, here is where to find me.
data.address: The old typewriter shop, 214 Wall Street, Kingston, NY 12401 — by appointment
data.hours: Studio by appointment, Tuesday to Friday, 10am to 4pm

metadata.tone: minimal, evocative, quietly confident, warm, editorial
metadata.structureNotes: The home page deliberately opens on a chrome-free fullImage hero and leads with images (gallery -> single minimal quote -> short about -> inquiry), skipping the generic services/testimonials/CTA stack so the work breathes; all commercial depth (stats, services, testimonials, pricing, FAQ) is pushed to the portfolio and inquire pages where a serious couple goes to decide.
